name: Belt (Equator First)
description: An unconventional method that begins by solving the four middle-layer (equatorial) edges first, forming a 'belt' or ring around the cube's equator. After the belt is formed, the top and bottom layers are solved. This approach inverts the typical layer-by-layer logic and offers a fresh perspective on the cube, though it is generally less efficient than mainstream methods.

steps.name: Belt / Equator Edges
steps.description: Solve the four edges of the middle layer (E-slice), forming a complete ring or belt around the equator of the cube. Each edge must be placed in its correct position with correct orientation.
tips: Identify all four E-slice edges before starting
Insert edges one at a time without disturbing solved ones
This step is similar to placing middle layer edges in beginner methods

steps.name: First Face Corners and Edges
steps.description: Solve all corners and edges of one face (top or bottom) while preserving the belt. This involves placing four edges and four corners into their correct positions.
tips: Work on edges first, then corners
Use algorithms that preserve the middle layer
Cross-like edge placement can help structure this step

steps.name: Last Face Edges
steps.description: Solve the four edges of the remaining face. Since the belt and one full face are solved, these edges must be placed carefully to avoid disruption.
tips: Limit moves to those that preserve solved layers
Similar techniques to last layer edge solving

steps.name: Last Face Corners
steps.description: Orient and permute the final four corners to complete the solve. This is the last step and can use OLL/PLL corner subsets or dedicated algorithms.
tips: Standard corner OLL and PLL algorithms can be adapted
Sune and A-perm variants are commonly used
Practice recognition from this unusual solving angle

pros: Unique and interesting approach to the cube
Middle layer is solved early, providing a visual anchor
cons: Later steps are constrained by the need to preserve the belt
Less ergonomic than layer or block methods
